summary: GitLab approved a restructuring plan expected to impact about 14% of its global workforce, with reporting putting the cut at roughly 350 employees, while CEO Bill Staples framed the move around GitLab's Act 2 strategy for the agentic era and AI-powered ways of working.
evidenceQuote: As we shift to a new strategy and way of working, powered by AI, we must revisit the size of staffing for each role to ensure we are optimizing for speed and customer outcomes.
notes: GitLab's June 2 Form 8-K says the board approved the plan on June 1, 2026, and that approximately 14% of the global workforce as of January 31, 2026 may be impacted. The same filing says GitLab expects to exit 22 countries and incur $30 million to $35 million in pre-tax restructuring charges. The layoff count is recorded as 350 based on Reuters-syndicated reporting and The Next Web's coverage of the WSJ-reported AI pivot. Because GitLab's CEO letter explicitly ties the restructuring to the agentic era, AI agents, and staffing right-sizing for AI-powered work, this is classified as explicit AI cited.

sources.name: GitLab Form 8-K
sources.quote: The Company anticipates approximately 14% of its global workforce as of January 31, 2026 may be impacted by the Plan.

sources.name: GitLab CEO letter / Form 8-K exhibit
sources.quote: As we shift to a new strategy and way of working, powered by AI, we must revisit the size of staffing for each role to ensure we are optimizing for speed and customer outcomes.

sources.name: Investing.com / Reuters
sources.quote: GTLB on Tuesday said it would reduce its full-time workforce by 350 employees, or about 14%.

sources.name: The Next Web
sources.quote: GitLab is cutting about 14% of its full-time workforce, roughly 350 people, and pulling out of 22 countries.
